[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>

It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 4, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995 * ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 3),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988 * ''Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 2),'' Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Transition from Myth to History,'' [[Chūōkōronsha]], 1965 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Emperor and Ancient Power,'' [[Iwanami Gendai Bunko]], 2000 * Kishi Toshio (Ed.), ''Conflict over Ancient Japanese Power,'' [[Chūkō Bunko]], 1996
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} * {{ill|Revolt of the Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏の乱}} * {{ill|Miyake (ancient Japan)|ja|屯倉}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} * {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}} * {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} * {{ill|Revolt of Iwai|ja|磐井の乱}} * {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}} * {{ill|Kibi no Kaefu Nanba|ja|吉備海部難波}}

He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). revision.text:
[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 4, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995 * ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 3),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988 * ''Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 2),'' Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Transition from Myth to History,'' [[Chūōkōronsha]], 1965 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Emperor and Ancient Power,'' [[Iwanami Gendai Bunko]], 2000 * Kishi Toshio (Ed.), ''Conflict over Ancient Japanese Power,'' [[Chūkō Bunko]], 1996
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} * {{ill|Revolt of the Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏の乱}} * {{ill|Miyake (ancient Japan)|ja|屯倉}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} * {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}} * {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} * {{ill|Revolt of Iwai|ja|磐井の乱}} * {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}} * {{ill|Kibi no Kaefu Nanba|ja|吉備海部難波}}

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 4, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995 * ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 3),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988 * ''Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 2),'' Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Transition from Myth to History,'' [[Chūōkōronsha]], 1965 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Emperor and Ancient Power,'' [[Iwanami Gendai Bunko]], 2000 * Kishi Toshio (Ed.), ''Conflict over Ancient Japanese Power,'' [[Chūkō Bunko]], 1996
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} * {{ill|Revolt of the Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏の乱}} * {{ill|Miyake (ancient Japan)|ja|屯倉}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} * {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}} * {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} * {{ill|Revolt of Iwai|ja|磐井の乱}} * {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}} * {{ill|Kibi no Kaefu Nanba|ja|吉備海部難波}}

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 4, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995 * ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 3),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988 * ''Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 2),'' Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Transition from Myth to History,'' [[Chūōkōronsha]], 1965 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Emperor and Ancient Power,'' [[Iwanami Gendai Bunko]], 2000 * Kishi Toshio (Ed.), ''Conflict over Ancient Japanese Power,'' [[Chūkō Bunko]], 1996
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} * {{ill|Revolt of the Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏の乱}} * {{ill|Miyake (ancient Japan)|ja|屯倉}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} * {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}} * {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} * {{ill|Revolt of Iwai|ja|磐井の乱}} * {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}} * {{ill|Kibi no Kaefu Nanba|ja|吉備海部難波}}

revision.text:
[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 4, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995 * ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 3),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988 * ''Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 2),'' Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Transition from Myth to History,'' [[Chūōkōronsha]], 1965 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Emperor and Ancient Power,'' [[Iwanami Gendai Bunko]], 2000 * Kishi Toshio (Ed.), ''Conflict over Ancient Japanese Power,'' [[Chūkō Bunko]], 1996
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} * {{ill|Revolt of the Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏の乱}} * {{ill|Miyake (ancient Japan)|ja|屯倉}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} * {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}} * {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} * {{ill|Revolt of Iwai|ja|磐井の乱}} * {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}} * {{ill|Kibi no Kaefu Nanba|ja|吉備海部難波}}

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>

It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条</ref>

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31st year 4月2日条</ref>

[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2nd year 12月29日条、神護景雲2nd year 5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31st year 4月2日条</ref>

revision.text:
[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30th year 1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2nd year 12月29日条、神護景雲2nd year 5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31st year 4月2日条</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 4, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995 * ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 3),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988 * ''Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 2),'' Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Transition from Myth to History,'' [[Chūōkōronsha]], 1965 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Emperor and Ancient Power,'' [[Iwanami Gendai Bunko]], 2000 * Kishi Toshio (Ed.), ''Conflict over Ancient Japanese Power,'' [[Chūkō Bunko]], 1996
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} * {{ill|Revolt of the Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏の乱}} * {{ill|Miyake (ancient Japan)|ja|屯倉}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} * {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}} * {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} * {{ill|Revolt of Iwai|ja|磐井の乱}} * {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}} * {{ill|Kibi no Kaefu Nanba|ja|吉備海部難波}}

revision.text:
[[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 19th volume, it was established in [[555 AD]] during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}}, the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30th year 1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[574 AD]], the Ōomi {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}, which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2nd year 12月29日条、神護景雲2nd year 5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[Northern Wei]]'s {{ill|Equal-field system|ja|均田制}}, transmitted via {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31st year 4月2日条</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 4, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995 * ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 3),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988 * ''Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 2),'' Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Transition from Myth to History,'' [[Chūōkōronsha]], 1965 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Emperor and Ancient Power,'' [[Iwanami Gendai Bunko]], 2000 * Kishi Toshio (Ed.), ''Conflict over Ancient Japanese Power,'' [[Chūkō Bunko]], 1996
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏}} * {{ill|Revolt of the Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏の乱}} * {{ill|Miyake (ancient Japan)|ja|屯倉}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} * {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} * {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目}} * {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}} * {{ill|Revolt of Iwai|ja|磐井の乱}} * {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉}} * {{ill|Kibi no Kaefu Nanba|ja|吉備海部難波}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏|12=draft}} * {{ill|Revolt of the Kibi clan|ja|吉備氏の乱|12=draft}} * {{ill|Miyake (ancient Japan)|ja|屯倉|12=draft}} * {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|12=draft}} * {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇|12=draft}} * {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|12=draft}} * {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇|12=draft}} * {{ill|Soga no Iname|ja|蘇我稲目|12=draft}} * {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子|12=draft}} * {{ill|Revolt of Iwai|ja|磐井の乱|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kibi no Kaefu Nanba|ja|吉備海部難波|12=draft}}

revision.text:
[[ファイル:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|白猪屯倉跡碑（[[岡山県]][[真庭市]]五反）]] '''白猪屯倉'''（しらい の みやけ）は、[[吉備国]]に設置された[[大和朝廷]]の直轄地（[[屯倉]]）。
== 概要 == 『[[日本書紀]]』巻第十九によれば、[[555年]]、[[欽明天皇]]の時代に、[[蘇我稲目|蘇我大臣稲目]]（そが の おおおみ いなめ）・[[穂積磐弓]]臣（ほづみ の いわゆみ の おみ）らを派遣して吉備の五つの郡（こおり）に設置された、とある<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>。
[[569年]]には、[[田部]]（たべ）を置いてから十余年がたち、一定年齢に達しても「籍」（なのふみた、戸籍）から漏れ、「課」（えつき、課役）を免れるものが多かったという。そのため、「白猪田部(しらいのたべ）の丁（よほろ）の籍（ふみた）を定むべし」という目的で、[[王辰爾]]（おうじんに）の甥の[[白猪胆津|胆津]]（いつ）が派遣された<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年1月1日条</ref>。胆津は「白猪田部」の「丁者」（よほろ、壮丁）を検閲し、詔によって籍を定め「田戸」(たべ)を作成した。胆津はこの定籍の功により「白猪」(しらい）の氏と「[[史]]」（ふひと)の[[カバネ|姓]](かばね)を与えられ、「[[田令 (屯倉)|田令]]」(たつかい)となり、欽明天皇17年7月6日（556年）に[[児島郡]]に置かれた屯倉の「田令」である「[[葛城山田瑞子|葛城山田直瑞子]]」（かずらき の やまだ の あたい みつこ）の副（すけ）となった、という<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30年4月条</ref>。
さらに、『書紀』巻第二十によると、[[574年]]の[[敏達天皇]]の時代に[[大臣 (古代日本)|大臣]][[蘇我馬子]]が遣わされて、白猪屯倉と田部が増益され、田部の「名籍」（なのふむた）が白猪史胆津に授けられた、ともある<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>。
== 考証 == 白猪屯倉の場所については諸説があり、 #[[美作国]][[大庭郡]]（おおばぐん）、 #吉備北部山間部 #[[児島屯倉]]と同一のもの と言われてきている。
このうち、1.について述べると、古くから[[岡山県]][[真庭市]]（旧[[真庭郡]]（まにわぐん）[[久世町]]（くせちょう））の五反廃寺跡の地だとされてきた。『[[続日本紀]]』巻第二十七、巻第二十九に美作国「大庭郡」の人である「白猪臣大足」（しらい の おみ おおたり)、「白猪臣証人」（しらい の おみ あきと）がそれぞれ「大庭臣」の姓を賜った、とあるからである<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>。「大庭郡」は「真庭郡」の南部で、廃寺跡がその地域に収まるからである。同寺跡出土の瓦が[[高句麗]]系のもので、[[白鳳]]期間に遡るものがあること、よって古くから[[帰化人]]（[[渡来人]]）が居住していたことが知られている。
この地は[[岡山平野]]に注ぐ[[旭川 (岡山県)|旭川]]の上流であり、[[大和]]と[[出雲]]とを結ぶ、[[出雲街道]]が横切る交通上の要衝であった。『書紀』巻第十八には、[[安閑天皇]]2年（[[535年]]）、（[[磐井の乱]]で大和政権が得た）北九州の広汎な多くの屯倉と並んで、播磨国の[[揖保郡]]（いいほぐん）越部（こしべ）屯倉のことがあげられている<ref>『日本書紀』安閑天皇2年5月9日条</ref>。同じ記事を載せる『[[播磨国風土記]]』によると、安閑天皇の寵人（おもいびと）である但馬君小津（たじまのきみおつ）が姓を賜り「皇子代君」となった。彼が屯倉をこの地に設置し、故に「子代の村」といった。のちに「上野大夫」（かみつけのまえつぎみ）によって三十戸を結んだ時に「越部の里」と改名された、とある<ref>『播磨国風土記』揖保郡越部の里（皇子代の里）条</ref>
「越部屯倉」は現在の兵庫県たつの市新宮町（旧揖保郡[[新宮町 (兵庫県)|新宮町]]）越部に比定されており、[[谷岡武雄]]は、この地に中世の[[大徳寺]]領播磨小宅庄条坊坪付図により、特殊条里のあることを指摘している。さらに、この地は令制の[[駅家]]の置かれていたところであるが、美作の[[津山盆地]]へ向かう津山路が[[姫路市]]の草上駅で海道沿いに西へすすむ[[山陽道]]と別れて最初の駅である。美作路はさらに、[[兵庫県]][[佐用郡]][[佐用町]]（旧[[三日月町 (兵庫県)|三日月町]]）に推定される中川駅を通り、杉坂峠を越えて美作国に入り、[[津山市]]の総社あたりと推定される美作国府へといたるのだが、美作国府から出雲へ向かう出雲街道上に白猪屯倉が存在するのである。
「白猪屯倉」は、「児島屯倉」とともに、吉備国造の支配していた地帯を南北にはさむ形で設置されている。これは、大和政権が[[雄略天皇]]末期に吉備勢力の反抗を制圧し、[[継体天皇]]の末期に筑紫国造の叛乱を打破して、吉備国南北に直轄地を置き、吉備王権を孤立化し、制圧してゆくさまを窺わせる。
同時に、[[蘇我氏]]が主導する欽明天皇時代の政治において、律令制的な農民支配の先駆が見られるとも言える。中国[[南北朝時代 (中国)|北朝]]の均田制が[[高句麗]]を通じて伝来した可能性も考えられる。欽明天皇31年4月2日（[[570年]]）には、高麗（高句麗）の使節が越国に漂流し、敏達天皇の時代にかけて交渉を開始したという記述が、『日本書紀』巻第十九・巻第二十にかけて存在する<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条、4月条、5月条、7月1日条、7月条、同32年3月条、敏達天皇元年5月1日条、5月15日条、6月条、7月条、同2年5月3日条、7月1日条</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』(三)（四）、[[岩波文庫]]、1995年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳(下）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『続日本紀』全現代語訳(中）、講談社学術文庫、宇治谷孟：訳、1992年 * 『コンサイス日本人名辞典 改訂新版』p649（[[三省堂]]、1993年） * 『岩波日本史辞典』p609、監修：永原慶二、[[岩波書店]]、1999年 * 『日本の歴史1 神話から歴史へ』、[[井上光貞]]：著、[[中央公論社]]、1965年 * 『天皇と古代王権』、井上光貞：著、[[吉村武彦]]：編、[[岩波現代文庫]]、2000年 * 『日本の古代6 王権をめぐる争い』、[[岸俊男]]：編、[[中公文庫]]、1996年 * 『毎日グラフ別冊 古代史を歩く4吉備』、[[毎日新聞社]]、1987
== 関連項目 == *[[吉備氏]] *[[吉備氏の乱]] *[[屯倉]] *[[雄略天皇]] *[[安閑天皇]] *[[欽明天皇]] *[[敏達天皇]] *[[蘇我稲目]] *[[蘇我馬子]] *[[磐井の乱]] *[[児島屯倉]] *[[吉備海部難波]]

[[ファイル:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|白猪屯倉跡碑（[[岡山県]][[真庭市]]五反）]] '''白猪屯倉'''（しらい の みやけ）は、[[吉備国]]に設置された[[大和朝廷]]の直轄地（[[屯倉]]）。

さらに、『書紀』巻第二十によると、[[574年]]の[[敏達天皇]]の時代に[[大臣 (古代日本)|大臣]][[蘇我馬子]]が遣わされて、白猪屯倉と田部が増益され、田部の「名籍」（なのふむた）が白猪史胆津に授けられた、ともある<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3年10月9日条</ref>。
== 考証 == 白猪屯倉の場所については諸説があり、 #[[美作国]][[大庭郡]]（おおばぐん）、 #吉備北部山間部 #[[児島屯倉]]と同一のもの と言われてきている。
このうち、1.について述べると、古くから[[岡山県]][[真庭市]]（旧[[真庭郡]]（まにわぐん）[[久世町]]（くせちょう））の五反廃寺跡の地だとされてきた。『[[続日本紀]]』巻第二十七、巻第二十九に美作国「大庭郡」の人である「白猪臣大足」（しらい の おみ おおたり)、「白猪臣証人」（しらい の おみ あきと）がそれぞれ「大庭臣」の姓を賜った、とあるからである<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2年12月29日条、神護景雲2年5月3日条</ref>。「大庭郡」は「真庭郡」の南部で、廃寺跡がその地域に収まるからである。同寺跡出土の瓦が[[高句麗]]系のもので、[[白鳳]]期間に遡るものがあること、よって古くから[[帰化人]]（[[渡来人]]）が居住していたことが知られている。

「越部屯倉」は現在の兵庫県たつの市新宮町（旧揖保郡[[新宮町 (兵庫県)|新宮町]]）越部に比定されており、[[谷岡武雄]]は、この地に中世の[[大徳寺]]領播磨小宅庄条坊坪付図により、特殊条里のあることを指摘している。さらに、この地は令制の[[駅家]]の置かれていたところであるが、美作の[[津山盆地]]へ向かう津山路が[[姫路市]]の草上駅で海道沿いに西へすすむ[[山陽道]]と別れて最初の駅である。美作路はさらに、[[兵庫県]][[佐用郡]][[佐用町]]（旧[[三日月町 (兵庫県)|三日月町]]）に推定される中川駅を通り、杉坂峠を越えて美作国に入り、[[津山市]]の総社あたりと推定される美作国府へといたるのだが、美作国府から出雲へ向かう出雲街道上に白猪屯倉が存在するのである。
「白猪屯倉」は、「児島屯倉」とともに、吉備国造の支配していた地帯を南北にはさむ形で設置されている。これは、大和政権が[[雄略天皇]]末期に吉備勢力の反抗を制圧し、[[継体天皇]]の末期に筑紫国造の叛乱を打破して、吉備国南北に直轄地を置き、吉備王権を孤立化し、制圧してゆくさまを窺わせる。
同時に、[[蘇我氏]]が主導する欽明天皇時代の政治において、律令制的な農民支配の先駆が見られるとも言える。中国[[南北朝時代 (中国)|北朝]]の均田制が[[高句麗]]を通じて伝来した可能性も考えられる。欽明天皇31年4月2日（[[570年]]）には、高麗（高句麗）の使節が越国に漂流し、敏達天皇の時代にかけて交渉を開始したという記述が、『日本書紀』巻第十九・巻第二十にかけて存在する<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31年4月2日条、4月条、5月条、7月1日条、7月条、同32年3月条、敏達天皇元年5月1日条、5月15日条、6月条、7月条、同2年5月3日条、7月1日条</ref>。

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:白猪屯倉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q55526455]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 19th volume, it was established in [[:en:555 AD|555 AD]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]], the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[:en:569 AD|569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tabe|qq=|1=Tabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24886872}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oujin no Toki|qq=|1=Shinni Ou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15113838}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Itsu|qq=|1=Shirai no Itsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526461}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuhito|qq=|1=史|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412098}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30th year 1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[:en:574 AD|574 AD]], the Ōomi [[:en:Soga no Umako|Soga no Umako]] increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima no Miyake|qq=|1=Kojima no Miyake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526459}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2nd year 12月29日条、神護景雲2nd year 5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]]'s [[:en:Equal-field system|Equal-field system]], transmitted via [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]].<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31st year 4月2日条</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 4, [[Iwanami Bunko]], 1995 * ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 3),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1988 * ''Shoku Nihongi: Complete Modern Translation (Vol. 2),'' Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko, 1992 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Transition from Myth to History,'' [[:en:Chūōkōronsha|Chūōkōronsha]], 1965 * Inoue Mitsusada, ''The Emperor and Ancient Power,'' [[Iwanami Gendai Bunko]], 2000 * Kishi Toshio (Ed.), ''Conflict over Ancient Japanese Power,'' [[Chūkō Bunko]], 1996
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Kibi clan|Kibi clan]] * [[:en:Kibi Clan Rebellion|Revolt of the Kibi clan]] * {{ill|Miyake (ancient Japan)|ja|屯倉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyake (ancient Japan)|qq=|1=Miyake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11465761}} * [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] * [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]] * [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] * [[:en:Emperor Bidatsu|Emperor Bidatsu]] * [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] * [[:en:Soga no Umako|Soga no Umako]] * [[:en:Iwai Rebellion|Revolt of Iwai]] * {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima no Miyake|qq=|1=Kojima no Miyake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526459}} * {{ill|Kibi no Kaefu Nanba|ja|吉備海部難波|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Kaefu Nanba|qq=|1=Kibi no Ama no Naniwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526276}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oujin no Toki|1=Shinni Ou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15113838}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Itsu|1=Shirai no Itsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526461}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuhito|1=史|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412098}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30th year 1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[:en:574 AD|574 AD]], the Ōomi [[:en:Soga no Umako|Soga no Umako]] increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima no Miyake|1=Kojima no Miyake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526459}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2nd year 12月29日条、神護景雲2nd year 5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]]'s [[:en:Equal-field system|Equal-field system]], transmitted via [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]].<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31st year 4月2日条</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:白猪屯倉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q55526455]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 19th volume, it was established in [[:en:555 AD|555 AD]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]], the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[:en:569 AD|569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tabe|1=Tabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24886872}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oujin no Toki|1=Shinni Ou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15113838}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Itsu|1=Shirai no Itsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526461}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuhito|1=史|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412098}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30th year 1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[:en:574 AD|574 AD]], the Ōomi [[:en:Soga no Umako|Soga no Umako]] increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima no Miyake|1=Kojima no Miyake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526459}}

It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]]'s [[:en:Equal-field system|Equal-field system]], transmitted via [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]].<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31st year 4月2日条</ref>

== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]]'s [[:en:Equal-field system|Equal-field system]], transmitted via [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]].<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31st year 4月2日条</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:白猪屯倉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q55526455]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:白猪屯倉遺阯碑.JPG|thumb|280px|right|Monument marking the site of Shirai no Miyake (Gotan, Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture)]] '''Shirai no Miyake''' (白猪屯倉) was an imperial estate (屯倉) established in [[:en:Kibi Province|Kibi Province]] under the direct control of the [[Yamato Court]].
== Overview == According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 19th volume, it was established in [[:en:555 AD|555 AD]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]. The court dispatched [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]], the Ōomi (Great Minister), and Hozumi no Iwayumi Omi to set up the estate in five districts of Kibi Province.<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇16年7月4日条</ref>
By [[:en:569 AD|569 AD]], about ten years after the establishment of the {{ill|Tabe|ja|田部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tabe|1=Tabe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24886872}} (administrative farming groups), many individuals of age were avoiding registration in the family registers and evading taxation. As a result, {{ill|Oujin no Toki|ja|王辰爾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oujin no Toki|1=Shinni Ou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15113838}}'s nephew, {{ill|Shirai no Itsu|ja|白猪胆津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirai no Itsu|1=Shirai no Itsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526461}}, was sent to oversee the Shirai Tabe. He conducted inspections and implemented family registrations for taxation purposes, creating official registers known as "田戸" (Tabe households). For his contributions, Shirai no Itsu was granted the Shirai surname and the kabane (hereditary title) of {{ill|Fuhito|ja|史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuhito|1=史|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412098}} (scribe).<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇30th year 1月1日条</ref>
In the reign of [[Emperor Bidatsu]], around [[:en:574 AD|574 AD]], the Ōomi [[:en:Soga no Umako|Soga no Umako]] increased the scope of Shirai no Miyake and its Tabe workers, granting the official family registers of Shirai to Shirai no Itsu.<ref>『日本書紀』敏達天皇3rd year 10月9日条</ref>
== Historical Verification == There are several theories about the location of Shirai no Miyake: 1. Ōba District (modern-day Maniwa City, Okayama Prefecture) 2. Mountainous areas in northern Kibi Province 3. Overlap with {{ill|Kojima no Miyake|ja|児島屯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima no Miyake|1=Kojima no Miyake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55526459}}
The most prominent theory links it to the Gotan temple ruins in Maniwa City, formerly part of the ancient Ōba District. This is supported by records in the [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]], which mention figures such as Shirai no Omi Ōtari and Shirai no Omi Akito, associated with Ōba District.<ref>『続日本紀』天平神護2nd year 12月29日条、神護景雲2nd year 5月3日条</ref>
Artifacts from the Gotan temple site, such as roof tiles resembling those of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], and the location's proximity to the strategic [[Izumo Kaidō]], reinforce the connection between Shirai no Miyake and ancient Yamato governance.
== Regional and Political Context == Shirai no Miyake, along with Kojima no Miyake, was strategically placed to isolate and weaken the power of the Kibi ruling authority, a measure intensified during the reigns of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]] and [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]]. It reflects the Yamato court's evolving governance practices, possibly influenced by [[:en:Northern Wei|Northern Wei]]'s [[:en:Equal-field system|Equal-field system]], transmitted via [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]].<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇31st year 4月2日条</ref>
